did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), a deep learning algorithm, to classify dermoscopic skin images as benign or malignant for melanoma detection. The model was trained on a Kaggle dataset of 9,605 images and evaluated on an independent test set, achieving an accuracy of 92.08%, precision of 91.60%, recall of 93.36%, and an F1-score of 92.47%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as a 'Research Article' in the header. It presents original experimental work involving the development, training, and validation of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model using a Kaggle dataset of 9,605 images. The study includes specific methods, results (accuracy, loss, confusion matrix), and the integration of the model into a mobile application, which constitutes new empirical results rather than a summary of prior literature.

title: Melanoma Skin Cancer Detection Application using Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)
journal: Journal of Information Systems Engineering and Management
brief_summary: This study developed a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to detect melanoma in dermoscopic skin images, addressing the need for accessible diagnostic tools in regions with limited dermatologist availability. The model was trained on a Kaggle dataset comprising 9,605 images (5,000 benign and 4,605 malignant) and evaluated on an independent test set of 1,000 images. The CNN achieved a final accuracy of 92.08%, with a precision of 91.60%, recall of 93.36%, and an F1-score of 92.47% over 20 epochs. To facilitate real-world application, the trained model was optimized using TensorFlow Lite and integrated into an Android mobile application. The resulting application provides users with a preliminary assessment of melanoma risk, aiming to improve early diagnosis and health equity for at-risk populations.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on detecting melanoma skin cancer. The dataset contains 4,605 malignant images (melanoma) out of 9,605 total images. The model classifies images into 'benign' or 'malignant' categories, with malignant corresponding to melanoma.

The study includes benign lesions as the negative class for melanoma detection. The dataset contains 5,000 benign images. The model distinguishes between benign (healthy) skin and malignant (melanoma) lesions.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of dermoscopic skin images into 'benign' or 'malignant' (melanoma) categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the CNN is designed for a binary classification problem to classify images into two categories: melanoma and non-melanoma. The model uses a softmax function to provide probability scores for these two classes, and the final layer (dense_7) has two units. The study evaluates performance using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and a confusion matrix for benign vs. malignant classes.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Melanoma Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from Kaggle containing dermoscopic skin images used for training and validation. The study also utilized a separate independent test set of 1,000 images for evaluation. Additionally, real-time testing was performed using actual images sourced from fisherfolk (the focus group) to evaluate performance in a practical environment.

use.training: Used to train the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model for pattern recognition and feature extraction.
use.validation: Used to monitor model performance and overfitting during training (loss and accuracy metrics reported).
use.testing: Used to evaluate the model's diagnostic effectiveness using an independent set of 1,000 images (500 benign, 500 malignant) to calculate accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.
use.other: Real-time images from fisherfolk were used for practical environment evaluation of the mobile application.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate and report performance metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score for the binary classification of benign versus malignant melanoma cases.
To quantify the difference between predicted and true probability distributions during training and to monitor model convergence and overfitting via training and validation loss plots.
To monitor the model's learning progress and generalization capability over 20 training epochs by comparing training and validation metrics.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrix analysis.

CNN (Convolutional Neural Network).validation_loss_fluctuations: Sharp increase in epoch 3 (0.4982) and epoch 16 (0.3889)

Kaggle Melanoma Dataset (Training/Valida.notes: Training and validation accuracy improved over 20 epochs, converging to 0.9044 and 0.9096 respectively. Training and validation loss decreased from 0.6548/0.4521 to 0.2235/0.2806 respectively.

Independent Test Set (1,000 images).notes: The model was evaluated on an independent test set of 1,000 images (500 benign, 500 malignant). The final accuracy was 92.08%.

Real-world Application (Fishersfolk).notes: Real-time testing was performed using actual images sourced from fisherfolk to evaluate performance in a practical environment. The application provides preliminary assessment of melanoma risk.

conclusions: The study successfully developed and integrated a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model for melanoma detection into an Android mobile application using TensorFlow Lite.
The CNN model demonstrated reliable performance for melanoma prediction, achieving a training accuracy of 90.44% and a validation accuracy of 90.96% over 20 epochs.

The application provides a preliminary assessment of melanoma risk, aiming to facilitate early diagnosis and improve health equity for at-risk populations with limited access to dermatologists, such as fisherfolk in the Philippines.

The authors acknowledge limitations regarding the dataset size and suggest that future work should focus on enhancing model performance through the use of expanded datasets and alternative algorithms.
important_remarks: The study relies on a Kaggle dataset (9,605 images) which may introduce selection bias and lacks diversity in skin tones, potentially limiting generalizability to populations with darker skin or different ethnic backgrounds.
The model is relatively large (19,264,322 parameters) despite using a small dataset, raising concerns about overfitting and the need for regularization, although dropout was employed.
Validation loss showed notable fluctuations (e.g., sharp increases in epochs 3 and 16), indicating potential instability during training that warrants further investigation.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals, relying solely on descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score).

The independent test set was artificially balanced (500 benign, 500 malignant), which does not reflect the typical prevalence of melanoma in clinical settings and may overestimate performance in real-world screening.
Real-world validation was limited to a small, non-representative focus group of fisherfolk in the Philippines, lacking broad external clinical validation across diverse demographics.
The application is positioned as a 'preliminary assessment' tool rather than a diagnostic device, highlighting the importance of clinical consultation for any positive results.
The authors acknowledge limitations regarding dataset size and suggest future work should focus on expanded datasets and alternative algorithms to enhance performance.
